coredata.dc:title: Determination of acrylamide in food products based on the fluorescence enhancement induced by distance increase between functionalized carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Qingyi
Liu, Ting
Pu, Hongbin
Sun, Da-Wen
coredata.dc:description: Acrylamide (AM), as one of the by-products produced by the Maillard reaction, has received increasing attention due to the potential risk of side effects in humans and other animals. In this study, a simple method was developed for AM quantitation by utilizing the fluorescence enhancement induced by the distance increase between functionalized carbon quantum dots modified by N-acryloxysuccinimide (NAS-CQDs) induced by AM polymerization. This fluorescent sensing approach allowed for detecting AM in a double-distilled water system in the range between 5 × 10−3 and 1 × 10−6 M with a low detection limit of 2.6 × 10−7 M. Furthermore, a novel method for the pretreatment of white bread crust samples was developed based on QuECHERS method (Quick, Easy, Cheap, Effective, Rugged and Safe), and detecting range between 5 × 10−4 and 5 × 10−6 M, low detection limit of 8.1 × 10−7 M, and satisfactory recoveries of 99.13–103.7% in spiked white bread crust samples were obtained by the present method, confirming its good applicability for AM determination in real food products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acrylamide
Carbon quantum dots
N-acryloxysuccinimide
Polymerization reaction

Optimization of the detection conditions for AM
Detection of AM in dd water
Pretreatment of white bread crust samples
Detection of AM in the extract of white bread crust
Effect of AP concentration on the sensor function
Effect of heating temperature and time on the sensor function
Measurements of AM in white bread crust

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to the Key Research and Development Program of Guangdong Province ( 2019B020212001 ) for its support. This research was also supported by the National Key R&D Program of China ( 2018YFC1603400 ), the Contemporary International Collaborative Research Centre of Guangdong Province on Food Innovative Processing and Intelligent Control ( 2019A050519001 ), the Common Technical Innovation Team of Guangdong Province on Preservation and Logistics of Agricultural Products ( 2019KJ145 , 2019KJ101 ), the Innovation Centre of Guangdong Province for Modern Agricultural Science and Technology on Intelligent Sensing and Precision Control of Agricultural Product Qualities and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( 2018MS056 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic diagram of the AM detection based on the distance change between NAS-CQDs induced by AM polymerization. (B) TEM images of pure NAS-CQDs (a), NAS-CQDs after a water bath at 65 °C for 40 min, where the concentration of AP solution and AM standard solution is 3‰ (m/v, dd water) and 0 M, respectively (b), and NAS-CQDs after a water bath at 65 °C for 40 min, where the concentration of AP solution and AM standard solution is 3‰ (m/v, dd water) and 1 × 10−5 M, respectively (c).

(A) TEM image of the synthesized CQDs, and the inset of shows the particle size distribution histogram. (B) The three-dimensional excitation-emission-matrix spectra of CQDs. (C) UV-vis absorption (black line) and fluorescence excitation (red line) spectra of the aqueous dispersion of CQDs, and the inset is the pictures of CQDs under natural light and UV lamp (365 nm). (D) FTIR spectra of CQDs. (E) The fluorescence spectra of the CQDs (black line) and NAS-CQDs (red line) with excitation wavelength at 350 nm. (F) Zeta potential of CQDs and NAS-CQDs. (G) UV-vis absorption CQDs (black line) and NAS-CQDs (red line). Tested CQDs and NAS-CQDs were at the same concentration (1.5 μg/mL). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Optimization of the detection conditions. (A, B) Effect of AP concentration (1-5‰, m/v, dd water) on the sensor function, where the fixed conditions were 65 °C of heating temperature and 40 min of heating time. (C, D) Effect of heating temperature (35-75 °C) on the sensor function, where the fixed conditions were 3‰ (m/v, dd water) of AP solution and 40 min of heating time. (E, F) Effect of heating time (0-100 min) on the sensor function, where the fixed conditions were 3‰ (m/v, dd water) of AP solution and 65 °C of heating temperature. Where ΔF = F-F0, F is the fluorescence intensity of NAS-CQDs with AM of
5 ×
10−3 M (i.e. the result of experimental group), and F0 is the fluorescence intensity of NAS-CQDs without AM (i.e. the result of blank group).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of NAS-CQDs with concentrations of AM ranging from 0 to
5 ×
10−8 M in dd water system, where the reaction conditions were 3‰ (m/v, dd water) of AP solution, 65 °C of heating temperature and 40 min of heating time. (B) Linear plot between ΔF (relative fluorescence intensities) and the logarithm values of AM concentrations ranging from 5 × 10−3 to 1 × 10−6 M, where ΔF = F-F0, F is the fluorescence intensity of NAS-CQDs at 442 nm with different concentrations of AM, F0 is the fluorescence intensity of NAS-CQDs at 442 nm without AM. Inset shows the pictures of pure NAS-CQDs under UV lamp with 365 nm (a), NAS-CQDs with 3‰ (m/v, dd water) of AP solution and different concentrations of AM (0,
5 ×
10−3 M,
5 ×
10−5 M from b to d) after a water bath at 65 °C for 40 min. Error bars from three independent measurements indicate the standard deviations. (C) Relative fluorescence intensities of NAS-CQDs without and with AM (1 × 10−5 M) in the 10 parallel detection systems at the emission wavelength of 442 nm. (D) Efficiency of fluorescence in the presence of AM (
1 ×
10−5 M) or interfering substances of BA, EACA, AA, C3, Gly, L-Asp, Suc, and Ca2+ solution (
1 ×
10−4 M), where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities at 442 nm without and with each agent, respectively. (E) Fluorescence spectra of NAS-CQDs with concentrations of AM ranging from 0 to
5 ×
10−6 M in white bread crust samples, where the reaction conditions were 3‰ (m/v, dd water) of AP solution, 65 °C of heating temperature and 40 min of heating time. (F) Linear plot between ΔF (relative fluorescence intensities) and the logarithm values of spiked AM concentrations ranging from 5 × 10−4 to 5 × 10−6 M, where ΔF = F-F0, F is the fluorescence intensity of NAS-CQDs at 442 nm with different concentrations of AM, F0 is the fluorescence intensity of NAS-CQDs at 442 nm without AM. Error bars from three independent measurements indicate the standard deviations.

SEM images for the cross-section of dried polyacrylamide obtained from 1 M AM (A), 0.8 M AM (B) and 0.6 M AM (C). The polymerization conditions were 3‰ (m/v, dd water) of AP solution, 65 °C of heating temperature and 40 min of heating time.
Comparison of the current work with previously reported sensors and accuracy of the current work in detecting AM in white bread crust samples.

row.entry: Fluorescent sensor based on gold nanoparticles and FAM-labeled double-stranded DNA

head.ce:title: Determination of acrylamide in food products based on the fluorescence enhancement induced by distance increase between functionalized carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, 510641, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Food Science and Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510641, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Acrylamide (AM), as one of the by-products produced by the Maillard reaction, has received increasing attention due to the potential risk of side effects in humans and other animals. In this study, a simple method was developed for AM quantitation by utilizing the fluorescence enhancement induced by the distance increase between functionalized carbon quantum dots modified by N-acryloxysuccinimide (NAS-CQDs) induced by AM polymerization. This fluorescent sensing approach allowed for detecting AM in a double-distilled water system in the range between 5 × 10−3 and 1 × 10−6 M with a low detection limit of 2.6 × 10−7 M. Furthermore, a novel method for the pretreatment of white bread crust samples was developed based on QuECHERS method (Quick, Easy, Cheap, Effective, Rugged and Safe), and detecting range between 5 × 10−4 and 5 × 10−6 M, low detection limit of 8.1 × 10−7 M, and satisfactory recoveries of 99.13–103.7% in spiked white bread crust samples were obtained by the present method, confirming its good applicability for AM determination in real food products.

ce:section.ce:para: Acrylamide (AM) has been classified as a potential carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) since 1994 [1]. In April 2002, researchers from the Swedish National Food Administration and Stockholm University first discovered AM in potato chips, toasted bread, and roast coffee, which has attracted worldwide attention [2]. AM is a by-product formed by the Maillard reaction, which occurs during heat processing of foods rich in carbohydrates at temperatures above 120 °C [3,4]. In recent years, many reports have revealed that consumption of AM could lead to genotoxic damage, neurotoxic effect, carcinogenic potential, and even gene mutation [5,6]. Therefore, it is particularly important to monitor and control the content of AM in foods.

To date, many analytical methods have been developed to determine the AM amount in foods, including gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) after bromination, liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) and liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [7,8]. Although these traditional detection methods show good performance in terms of accuracy [9–12], they generally require complicated sample pre-processing and expensive instrumentation. In addition, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) for measuring AM in foods has also been developed in recent years [13,14], but it could suffer from low accuracy due to lack of antibodies with strong affinity. In recent years, many spectroscopic and imaging techniques have showed promising performance in rapid evaluation of food quality and safety [15–24]. Among them, some alternative sensitive and simple methods based on nanomaterials for AM detection have been presented.. Hu et al. [25] proposed a fluorescent protocol for the determination of AM through the special property of quantum dots (QDs). Gezer et al. [26] developed a biodegradable zein-based sensor with surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) for the selective detection of AM in food samples. Besides, Mn-doped ZnS QDs has been used to measure the amount of AM in baked foods [27]. Furthermore, Cheng et al. [28] successfully applied re-oxidized graphene oxide/Au nanoparticle composites and dispersive solid-phase extraction through SERS for detecting AM in foods, and Asnaashari et al. Asnaashari et al. [29] constructed a simple and fluorescent sensor for the rapid sensing of AM based on gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) and FAM-labeled double-stranded DNA (FAM-dsDNA). However, up to now, the application of fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for AM detection in the complicated food system has never been reported.

CQDs are a new kind of excellent optical nanomaterials, with advantages such as environmental friendliness, low toxicity, low cost, simple synthetic routes, and water solubility [30,31]. Because of these unique properties, CQDs have been used as fluorescence probes for detecting food additives and harmful substances, such as foodborne pathogens [32], tartrazine [33], tocopherol in rice [34], and mycotoxins [35].

It has been reported that fluorescence intensity is very sensitive to the distance change between CQDs [36,37]. Therefore, the current study proposed an inexpensive fluorescent approach for AM quantification based on the distance change between CQDs induced by AM polymerization. Results showed that the present analytical method offered a convenient and sensitive fluorescent approach for AM measurement with a wide linear range, low detection limit, and satisfactory recoveries. Besides, the favourable feasibility of the current approach was also demonstrated through the detection of AM in artificially spiked white bread crust samples.

Citric acid, ethylenediamine, N-acryloxysuccinimide (NAS), phosphate buffered saline (PBS, 1 M, pH 8.0), AM, and ammonium persulphate (AP) were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Butyric acid (BA), 6-aminocaproic acid (EACA), propionic acid (C3), acetic acid (AA), Ca2+ solution (100 μg/mL, 5% HNO3), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), methanol, and formic acid were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glycine (Gly), L-aspartic acid (L-Asp), sucrose (Suc), hexane, and acetonitrile were bought from a local company (Jingke Biotech Co., Ltd, Guangzhou, China). QuECHERS pouches (containing 50 mg primary secondary amine (PSA), 50 mg HC-C18, and 150 mg MgSO4) were supplied by Agilent Technologies Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). All the other chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and used directly with no further purification. All experiments were carried out in double-distilled water (dd water), which was obtained from A.S. Watson Group Ltd. (Hong Kong, China).

The CQDs were prepared using a one-step hydrothermal method [38] using a high throughput closed microwave digestion/extraction workstation with twelve high-pressure reaction tanks (Jupiter-b, Sineo Microwave Chemistry Technology Co., Shanghai, China). An amount of 2 g of citric acid and 0.954 mL of ethylenediamine were added to 20 mL of dd water, respectively, which was stirred until the precipitate was dissolved. The transparent mixture was then transferred into one tank and heated at 200 °C for 5 min. After that, the reaction tank was cooled naturally to room temperature of 25 °C. The resultant CQDs solution was filtered through a microporous membrane with a pore diameter of 0.22 μm to remove any large fragments. The solution was then purified through a dialyzer tube (MWCO, 100-500Da, Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Shanghai, China) for 48 h to remove unreacted moieties such as citric acid and ethylenediamine. Finally, the brown-yellow aqueous solution containing CQDs was dried by a freeze dryer (SCIENTZ-18N, Xinzhi Bioscience Co., Inc, Ningbo, China) for 40 h to obtain the dried powder.

The three-dimensional excitation-emission-matrix spectra of the CQDs were obtained by an RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Kyoto, Japan) equipped with a xenon lamp and a quartz carrier. The UV-vis spectra of the CQDs were acquired by a UV-vis spectrophotometer (UV-1800, Shimadzu Co., Kyoto, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the CQDs were obtained by a Tensor infrared spectrophotometer (Nicolet-iS50, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, USA). The surface morphology of the CQDs was characterized by a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM-1400 Plus, JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of functional CQDs with NAS
ce:section.ce:para: The NAS-CQDs solution was prepared just before use. Briefly, 400 μL of the NAS solution (25 mM, dissolved by DMSO) was added into 3.6 mL of the CQDs solution (1.5 μg/mL, diluted by PBS (0.05 M, pH 8.0)). After mixed evenly, the mixture reacted in the dark for 2 h. Finally, the NAS-CQDs solution was obtained and temporarily stored at 4 °C for subsequent uses.

Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra, UV-vis spectra and Zeta potential of NAS-CQDs were obtained by the RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer, the UV-vis spectrophotometer and a potentiometric analyzer (Zetasizer Nano, Malvern Instruments Ltd., Melvin, UK), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the detection conditions for AM
ce:section.ce:para: An amount of 200 μL of the AM standard solution with 5 × 10−3 M was added into 2 mL of the prepared NAS-CQDs solution at room temperature. Next, 30 μL of the AP solution (1-5‰, m/v, dd water) was added to the solution, and the mixture was then heated in a water bath at 35-75 °C for 0-100 min. After reaching the heating time, the reaction was stopped immediately using the mixture of ice and water, then the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution after cooling was determined by the RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer under excitation at 350 nm and the slit width was set at 3.0 nm. Each calibration curve was derived from the average of three independent measurements (n = 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AM in dd water
ce:section.ce:para: An amount of 200 μL of the target samples (AM standard solution with gradient concentrations ranging from 0 to 5 × 10−8 M) was added into 2 mL of the prepared NAS-CQDs solution at room temperature. Next, 30 μL of the AP solution (3‰, m/v, dd water) was added to each solution, and the mixtures were then heated in a water bath at 65 °C for 40 min. The fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution was then measured using the same procedure described in Section 2.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pretreatment of white bread crust samples
ce:section.ce:para: White bread was purchased from a local supermarket (Guangzhou, China) and only white bread crust with attractive colour was selected as samples. Firstly, 2.0 g of the ground samples and hexane (10 mL) were placed in 50 mL centrifuge tube and subjected to sonication by an ultrasonic cleaning machine (SB-5200 DTDN, Xinzhi Biotech Co., Ltd., Ningbo, China) for 15 min. Next, 15 mL of the mixed extraction solvent of water and acetonitrile (v/v = 1:1) was added into the tube and agitated by a thermostatic oscillator (Changzhou Aohua Instrument Co., Ltd., Changzhou, China) for 10 min [28]. After that, the mixture in the tube was centrifuged using a high-speed refrigerated centrifuge (Anhui Jiaven Equipment Industry Co., Ltd., Hefei, China) for 20 min at 12,000 rpm and 4 °C to separate AM into the acetonitrile phase. Then the subnatant solution (5 mL) was transferred into a prepared mix of the QuECHERS pouch (50 mg PSA+50 mg HC-C18+150 mg MgSO4) and vibrated by the thermostatic oscillator for another 10 min, which allowed AM to be extracted from white bread crust samples as completely as possible. After centrifuging for 10 min at 10,000 rpm and 4 °C, the supernatant phase was collected, and its AM content was measured after 100-fold dilution before measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AM in the extract of white bread crust
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the application of the present sensing approach in real samples, 200 μL of the white bread crust extracts (Section 2.6) were spiked with concentrations ranging from 0 to 5 × 10−6 M, and added into 2 mL of the prepared NAS-CQDs solution (Section 2.3) at room temperature, followed by the addition of 30 μL of the AP solution (3‰, m/v, dd water). Then the mixed solutions were heated in a water bath at 65 °C for 40 min. The fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution was then measured using the same procedure described in Section 2.4.

In addition, in order to evaluate the accuracy of the present approach, the content of real white bread crust extracts with spiked concentrations of 7.5 × 10−4, 5 × 10−5, and 2.5 × 10−6 M AM was determined using the fluorescent sensor as described above and the standard LC-MS/MS method based on the GB 5009.204–2014 of China, respectively. LC-MS/MS detection conditions were as follows: Atlantis C18 chromatographic column (particle size = 5 μm, inner diameter = 2.1 mm, and length = 150 mm) (Agilient Technologies Co. Ltd, Shanghai, China) was applied for analytical separation at a column temperature of 26 °C. Isocratic elution was achieved with a mobile phase of methanol: 0.1% formic acid (v/v = 1:9) at a flow rate of 0.2 mL/min, and ionization mode of ESI+ and MS detection mode of MRM were then utilized for AM quantification (m/z, 72/55), where the ion source temperature, capillary voltage, fragmentor voltage and collision energy were 80 °C, 3500 V, 40 V and 6 eV, respectively.

The linear curve fitted using Origin Pro 8 (OriginLab Co., MA, USA) was applied to evaluate the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the proposed sensor and AM standard solutions or real white bread crust samples. Besides, the goodness-of-fit of the linear curve was estimated by the coefficient of determination (R2). The obtained results were presented as the average of three repetitions and the relative standard deviation (RSD, %) was also used to indicate the precision of the results.

The schematic diagram of the AM detection with NAS-CQDs is exhibited in Fig. 1 A. As shown in Fig. 1A, the CQDs were first synthesized by the microwave-assisted method, then NAS was modified to the surface of CQDs through amino groups in order to construct functional NAS-CQDs with CC bonds. Further, these CC bonds can participate in the polymerization reaction under experimental conditions [25,39]. When AM was absent in the system, the functional groups of NAS-CQDs polymerized with the assistance of initiator and heating, resulting in the distance between NAS-CQDs getting closer along with a drastic decrease in fluorescence intensity. However, when AM was simultaneously involved in the polymerization, the distance between NAS-CQDs became greater, with the fluorescence intensity increasing compared with that without AM. In addition, TEM images in Fig. 1B also supported the above principle.

From Fig. 1B (a), it could be seen that pure NAS-CQDs were evenly dispersed in the dd water system, where the average distance between NAS-CQDs was 12.58 nm. With the addition of 3‰ (m/v, dd water) of AP solution and 0 M of AM standard solution, NAS-CQDs polymerized with the assistance of water bath at 65 °C for 40 min, leading to the shortening of the distance between NAS-CQDs with an average length of 5.07 nm (Fig. 1B (b)). However, under the same conditions, when the concentration of AM standard solution was 1 × 10−5 M, the distance between NAS-CQDs became longer compared with that without AM, and the average distance was 7.47 nm (Fig. 1B (c)).

The TEM images were taken to study the morphology of the obtained CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the CQDs were nearly spherical in shape and dispersed evenly in dd water with an average particle diameter about 3.9 nm. Besides, the three-dimensional excitation-emission-matrix spectra of CQDs (Fig. 2B) reveals that the CQDs optimal excitation and emission wavelengths were at 350 nm and 445 nm, respectively. Additionally, the UV-vis absorption spectra of the CQDs were acquired to investigate the optical properties of CQDs. From Fig. 2C, it was seen that the CQDs exhibited a remarkable absorption peak at around 350 nm in the UV-vis absorption region, which could be related to the optimum fluorescence excitation peak (350 nm), and the UV-vis peak at 350 nm could be attributed to n-π* transition of CO bonds [40]. Another absorption band was at 242 nm, which could be ascribed to π-π* transition of aromatic CC bonds [41,42]. Simultaneously, Fig. 2C also displays that CQDs showed a blue colour under a hand-held UV lamp with a wavelength of 365 nm. The FTIR spectra were used to characterize the surface state of CQDs. As seen in Fig. 2D, the peaks at 1556 cm−1 and 3100-3300 cm−1 indicated the presence of bending and stretching vibrations of the N-H, respectively, while the peak at 1157 cm−1 could be attributed to the C-NH-C. Further, the existence of these amine groups paved the way for the subsequent preparation of functional CQDs with NAS, at the same time, the stretching vibrations of C-OH and CO at 3200-3600 cm−1 and 1654 cm−1 respectively illustrated good water solubility of the present CQDs [43,44].

In the current study, when NAS was added into CQDs dilution (pH 8.0), amino groups on the surface of CQDs reacted with NAS. After the reaction, the surface of CQDs was successfully modified with CC bonds and CO bonds (Fig. 1A). Fig. 2E shows the fluorescence spectrum of NAS-CQDs after reaction for 2 h in the dark. The fluorescence intensity of NAS-CQDs was reduced by 15% compared with CQDs with the same concentration, and accompanied by a spectral blue shift, which could be due to the electron transfer effect from CQDs to NAS [25]. In addition, Zeta potential results (Fig. 2F) showed that NAS-CQDs (-16.45 mV) had higher potential than CQDs (-30.3 mV) because the NAS structure was positively charged. Furthermore, in order to verify the modification of CQDs with NAS, NAS-CQDs were characterized by the UV-vis spectrophotometer. Fig. 2G reveals the results from UV-vis spectrophotometer. Compared with CQDs, the UV absorption of NAS-CQDs at 240 nm and 325-380 nm (-CO-, –CHCH-, and –C(=O)-NH-) changed significantly, demonstrating that NAS was successfully modified to the surface of CQDs [25,40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of AP concentration on the sensor function
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the optimum concentration of AP for polymerization reaction, various amounts of AP were added to the detecting system. After heating in the water bath at 65 °C for 40 min, the fluorescence intensities of NAS-CQDs with and without AM were measured (Fig. 3 A) and ΔF (= F-F0) (relative fluorescence intensity) is shown in Fig. 3B, where F is the fluorescence intensity of NAS-CQDs at 442 nm with AM (i.e., the result of the experimental group) and F0 is the fluorescence intensity of NAS-CQDs at 442 nm without AM (i.e., the result of the blank group). The results indicated that ΔF of NAS-CQDs increased gradually with increasing the concentration of AP for the experimental group from 1‰ to 3‰, and reached the highest value at the concentration of 3‰. However, a further increase in the amount to 5‰ caused a sharp decline in the fluorescence intensity, and even without polymerization, which might be due to excessive AP causing the quenching effects for NAS-CQDs [25]. Besides, excessive AP concentrations may also increase the molecular chain termination reaction rate, and even cause the disappearance of active chain activity [45]. Therefore, by comprehensive consideration of polymerization and the effect on the fluorescence, 3‰ of AP was chosen for sensitive detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of heating temperature and time on the sensor function
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the optimum heating temperature for sensor function, 3‰ of AP solution was added to the fix mixtures and then heated in the water bath at 35-75 °C for 40 min. As shown in Fig. 3C, the fluorescence intensity of the blank group (F0) and experimental group (F) decreased with increasing temperatures from 35 to 75 °C, which might be due to excessive free radicals produced by AP decomposition. AP is an initiator sensitive to temperature, and when the heating temperature gradually increases, the decomposition rate of AP also increases, which in turn produces more free radicals [45]. As the free radicals were fully contacted, AM polymerization and the quenching effects for NAS-CQDs took place. In addition, in Fig. 3D, ΔF of NAS-CQDs at 442 nm increased gradually until reaching the maximum level at the temperature of 65 °C, followed by a decrease with further increase in temperature above 65 °C, and thus 65 °C was the optimal heating temperature.

The effect of heating time on sensor function was also investigated (Fig. 3E and F). The results revealed that ΔF of NAS-CQDs at 442 nm gradually increased as heating time increasing from 0 to 30 min, and then gradually declined from 40 to 100 min to even negative value. Therefore, 40 min was adopted as the optimum heating time for the detecting system.

The interaction between AM and NAS-CQDs was investigated based on the fluorescence intensity change of NAS-CQDs. Fig. 4 A depicts the fluorescence emission spectra using NAS-CQDs probes to detect AM in different concentrations from 0 to 5 × 10−8 M in standard solutions. As the concentration of AM decreased within a certain range, the fluorescence intensity of the emission peak increased. In order to explore the reason for this phenomenon, three different concentrations of AM (1 M, 0.8 M, 0.6 M, respectively) were selected to polymerize without NAS-CQDs in the water bath (3‰ (m/v, dd water) of AP solution, 65 °C, 40 min). The cross-section of the samples freeze-dried for 36 h was characterized by a scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Carl Zeiss NTS GmbH, Oberkochen, Germany) and results revealed the cross-section of the polyacrylamide obtained from 1 M AM was layered without pore structure (Fig. 5 A). Gradually, the cross-section for 0.8 M AM began to have pore structure accompanied with branched chains (Fig. 5B), and that for 0.6 M AM exhibited a good pore structure (Fig. 5C). Similarly, if NAS-CQDs were added into the system, low-concentration AM would result in a greater distance between NAS-CQDs because of pore structure after polymerization than high-concentration AM, and thus the fluorescence intensity would reach a higher level.

In addition, it could be seen from Fig. 4B that there was a good linear relationship between ΔF and the logarithm values of AM concentrations ranging from 5 × 10−3 to 1 × 10−6 M with R2 of 0.995, which could be described as:
(1)
Δ F = - 23.06 ×
[ Log
( AM
concentration )
]
- 11.05

The limit of detection (LOD) can be determined by the following equation [46–48]:
(2)
LOD = 3
S 0
+
Y 0
where S0 is the standard deviation of the relative fluorescence intensity of the blank samples at 442 nm, and Y0 is the mean relative fluorescence intensity of the blank samples. The LOD was thus calculated as 2.6 × 10−7 M.

Table 1 compares the detection limits of AM and its linear range in the current research with those reported in the literature, showing the electrochemistry method and instrumental analysis have satisfactory LOD values, however, these methods have disadvantages including time-consuming sample preparation procedures, the need of trained technicians, high cost, and strict requirements on the solvent. On the other hand, among the fluorescence methods, the current work provided a simple and environmentally-friendly approach, which required no sophisticated analytical procedure and had a low LOD, demonstrating the outstanding performance in quantitative determination using the present fluorescent method.

In order to investigate the stability of the system, 10 parallel determinations were performed. Fig. 4C shows the relative fluorescence intensities of NAS-CQDs with and without AM (1 × 10−5 M) in the 10 parallel detection systems at the emission wavelength of 442 nm, with the relative standard deviation of 2.68%, indicating that the quantification of AM by NAS-CQDs had a good performance in terms of stability.

For evaluating the selectivity of the fluorescent probe, the fluorescence response of different interfering substances (BA, EACA, AA, C3, Gly, L-Asp, Suc, and Ca2+ solutions) was investigated following the same procedures as AM detection. All the interfering substances used in the experiments had the same concentration of 1 × 10−4 M, whereas the concentration of AM was 1 × 10−5 M. As shown in Fig. 4D, ΔF for lower-concentration AM was significantly higher than other interfering substances with higher-concentrations. These results demonstrated that the proposed method could be applied for AM measurements, also without any interference from other substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements of AM in white bread crust
ce:section.ce:para:
For the measurements of AM in real food samples, different concentrations of AM standards were spiked into the extract of the white bread crust to evaluate the performance of the fluorescent sensor. Fig. 4E depicts that the fluorescence changes of NAS-CQDs of the white bread crust extract spiked with different amounts of AM. Fig. 4F demonstrates that there was a linear relationship as given below between ΔF and the logarithm values of spiked AM concentrations ranging from 5 × 10−4 to 5 × 10−6 M, with R2 of 0.969 and LOD of 8.1 × 10−7 M:
(3)
Δ F = - 39.64 ×
( Log
( spiked
AM
concentration )
)
- 94.61
where ΔF = F-F0 with F and F0 being the fluorescence intensities of NAS-CQDs with and without spiked AM, respectively. The results confirmed that the current method could successfully be used for detecting AM in white bread crust samples.

In addition, the recovery assay was performed by spiking three amounts of AM (7.5 × 10−4 M, 5 × 10−5 M, and 2.5 × 10−6 M) into white bread crust. As also listed in Table 1, the relative standard deviation (RSD, %) of the repeated measurements ranged from 0.84 to 2.72%, providing that this fluorescent method had good precision. Besides, the recovery values of the fluorescent sensor ranging from 99.13 to 103.7% were similar to those of the standard LC-MS/MS method, revealing that the results obtained by the fluorescent probe were accurate and reliable.

In the current study, good performance CQDs were modified with NAS, and the developed functional NAS-CQDs were used as the fluorescent probe for the detection of AM with high sensitivity, selectivity and accuracy. The detection relied on the distance change between NAS-CQDs induced by AM polymerization. The present method showed good stability and reproducibility, and provided a broad linear relationship in the double-distilled water system with AM concentrations ranging from
5 ×
10−3 to
1 ×
10−6 M, achieving a low LOD of 2.6 × 10−7 M. Besides, a novel method for the pretreatment of white bread crust samples was developed based on QuECHERS, and the achieved detecting range between
5 ×
10−4 and
5 ×
10−6 M, LOD of 8.1 × 10−7 M, satisfactory recoveries and RSD values of the experimental results in white bread crust samples also indicated the feasibility for monitoring AM contents in baked food. Therefore, the proposed method should provide a simple, sensitive and effective way of detecting AM in other thermally processed foods.

Qingyi Wei: Supervision, Validation, Funding acquisition, Resources. Ting Liu: Writing - original draft, Formal analysis, Investigation. Hongbin Pu: Validation, Funding acquisition, Resources. Da-Wen Sun: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Resources, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the Key Research and Development Program of Guangdong Province (2019B020212001) for its support. This research was also supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (2018YFC1603400), the Contemporary International Collaborative Research Centre of Guangdong Province on Food Innovative Processing and Intelligent Control (2019A050519001), the Common Technical Innovation Team of Guangdong Province on Preservation and Logistics of Agricultural Products (2019KJ145, 2019KJ101), the Innovation Centre of Guangdong Province for Modern Agricultural Science and Technology on Intelligent Sensing and Precision Control of Agricultural Product Qualities and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2018MS056).
